topic: Unit 4: Personal or Creative Writing and Reading Literary and Non-fiction Texts
subject: english-language

items.q: What is the difference between voiced and voiceless sounds?
items.correct: Voiced sounds are produced with vocal-fold vibration (/b/, /d/, /g/, /z/); voiceless sounds without it (/p/, /t/, /k/, /s/). You can feel the difference by putting a hand on your throat.
items.misconception: Voiced sounds are loud and voiceless sounds are whispered.
items.random_wrong: Voiced sounds use the lips; voiceless sounds use the tongue.
items.why: Voicing is about vocal-fold vibration, not volume. Whisper has no voicing at all but isn't silent. Students confuse phonation with loudness, missing how voicing distinguishes minimal pairs (pin/bin).

items.q: What did Chomsky propose about language acquisition?
items.correct: Children possess an innate Language Acquisition Device — an inborn capacity for grammar — because exposure alone cannot account for how rapidly and uniformly children learn complex syntax.
items.misconception: Chomsky said children learn language entirely by copying adults.
items.random_wrong: Chomsky said children should be taught grammar from age two.
items.why: Chomsky's theory is precisely the opposite of behaviourist imitation. Students confuse Chomsky with Skinner — Chomsky's argument is that imitation cannot explain acquisition.

items.correct: A dialect that has gained social and institutional prestige — used in education and formal writing; it is one variety, not inherently superior in linguistic terms.
items.misconception: Standard English is the original and correct form of the language.
items.random_wrong: Standard English is the same as Received Pronunciation.
items.why: Standard English is one historical dialect (originally East Midlands) that gained prestige. It is not older or more logical than other varieties. Students confuse standard with RP (Standard is grammar/lexis; RP is accent).

items.q: What does the L in PEEL stand for?
items.why: Correct answer: Link. “List” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which reading skill hunts specific facts?
items.why: Correct answer: Scanning. “Skimming” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many letters in PEEL paragraph structure?
items.why: The real figure is 4 letters. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
